title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 9
section: R Exercise: Wisconsin Breast Cancer Classification
text:
R Exercise: Wisconsin Breast Cancer Classification






Exercise: Classic Benchmark Dataset


The Wisconsin Breast Cancer dataset is one of the most widely-used classification benchmarks in machine learning. It contains 699 observations of fine-needle aspirate (FNA) measurements from breast masses, classified as malignant or benign.

Load the data with data(BreastCancer, package = "mlbench")
Explore the class distribution — how balanced are the classes?
Train and compare a Random Forest (500 trees; examine variable importance)
Create a confusion matrix and calculate odds and log odds?
Why is sensitivity particularly important in cancer diagnosis (note - this is a seque into Bayesian analysis)?

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 9
text:
An Alternative Framework: Bayesian Statistics

So far we have used frequentist methods — parameters are fixed unknowns, and we use data to estimate them with confidence intervals and p-values.
There is an elegant alternative: Bayesian statistics, where parameters have probability distributions that are updated as we observe data.
This approach naturally incorporates prior knowledge and provides intuitive probability statements about parameters — “there is a 95% probability the effect is between 0.3 and 1.2” rather than the frequentist version which refers to hypothetical repeated samples.

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 9
text:
Frequentist vs. Bayesian
Frequentist (what we’ve learned so far):

Parameters are fixed, unknown constants
Probability = long-run frequency of events
Confidence intervals: “95% of such intervals contain the true parameter”
Focus on p-values and hypothesis tests

Bayesian:

Parameters have probability distributions
Probability = degree of belief
Credible intervals: “95% probability the parameter is in this range”
Focus on posterior distributions

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 9
text:
Bayes’ Theorem Review

EquationLaTeXComponentsInterpretation


\[P(\theta \mid data) = \frac{P(data \mid \theta) \cdot P(\theta)}{P(data)}\]
Or more simply: \[\text{Posterior} \propto \text{Likelihood} \times \text{Prior}\]


P(\theta \mid data) = \frac{P(data \mid \theta) \cdot P(\theta)}{P(data)}



Prior \(P(\theta)\): What we believe before seeing data
Likelihood \(P(data \mid \theta)\): How probable the data is given a parameter value
Posterior \(P(\theta \mid data)\): Updated belief after seeing data
The posterior is a compromise between the prior and the data




Bayes’ theorem provides a principled mechanism for updating beliefs: the posterior combines prior knowledge with observed data through the likelihood
The denominator \(P(data)\) serves as a normalizing constant ensuring the posterior integrates to 1 — in practice it is often computed implicitly rather than directly
The proportionality form (Posterior \(\propto\) Likelihood \(\times\) Prior) highlights that the posterior shape is determined entirely by the product of prior and likelihood
When the prior is “flat” (uninformative), the posterior is proportional to the likelihood alone, and Bayesian estimates converge to maximum likelihood estimates

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 9
text:
The Logic of Bayesian Inference







Source: Lecture material (SVG recreation)

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 9
text:
Bayesian Estimation: The Coin Flip
Setup: We have a possibly biased coin. We want to estimate \(\theta\) = probability of heads. We observe flips and update our belief about \(\theta\) after each batch of data.
Why the Beta distribution? We need a prior over a probability (0 to 1). The Beta distribution is the natural choice — it is the conjugate prior for the Binomial likelihood, meaning the posterior is also a Beta distribution (see next slides).
\[\theta \sim \text{Beta}(\alpha, \beta) \qquad \text{Prior belief about } \theta\]
\[\text{Data: } X \sim \text{Binomial}(n, \theta) \qquad \text{n flips, X heads}\]
\[\theta | X \sim \text{Beta}(\alpha + X, \;\beta + n - X) \qquad \text{Posterior}\]
The parameters \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\) can be thought of as pseudo-counts of prior “successes” and “failures” — they represent how many imaginary heads/tails we’ve already seen before collecting data.

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 9
text:
Bayesian Estimation: Coin Flip Example
Starting with a weakly informative prior Beta(2,2) — slight belief the coin is fair — we observe 7 heads in 10 flips:

OutputCodeInterpretation














theta <- seq(0, 1, length.out = 200)

# Prior: Beta(2, 2) — represents ~2 imaginary heads and 2 imaginary tails
prior <- dbeta(theta, 2, 2)

# Likelihood: 7 heads out of 10 flips
likelihood <- dbinom(7, 10, theta)

# Posterior: Beta(alpha + X, beta + n - X) = Beta(2+7, 2+3) = Beta(9, 5)
posterior <- dbeta(theta, 9, 5)

# Posterior summary
posterior_mean <- 9 / (9 + 5)
credible_interval <- qbeta(c(0.025, 0.975), 9, 5)
cat("Posterior mean:", round(posterior_mean, 3))
cat("95% Credible Interval:", round(credible_interval, 3))




The posterior mean (0.643) is a compromise between the prior mean (0.50 from Beta(2,2)) and the data proportion (0.70 = 7/10), pulled toward the prior because n=10 is small
The 95% credible interval gives a direct probability statement: there is a 95% probability the true coin bias lies within this range, given the data and prior
The Beta-Binomial conjugacy makes updating trivial: just add observed heads to \(\alpha\) and observed tails to \(\beta\) — no numerical integration required
With only 10 flips, the prior contributes meaningfully; with hundreds of flips, the posterior would be almost entirely determined by the data

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 9
section: Bayesian Updating: Effect of Sample Size
text:
Bayesian Updating: Effect of Sample Size
As we collect more data, the posterior becomes increasingly dominated by the likelihood — data overwhelms the prior.

Sequential UpdatingCodeKey TakeawayInterpretation














alpha_prior <- 2; beta_prior <- 2  # Prior: Beta(2, 2)
flip_counts <- c(5, 15, 50, 200)

for (n in flip_counts) {
  x <- sum(rbinom(n, 1, true_theta))  # Simulate flips
  alpha_post <- alpha_prior + x
  beta_post  <- beta_prior + (n - x)
  
  post_mean <- alpha_post / (alpha_post + beta_post)
  ci <- qbeta(c(0.025, 0.975), alpha_post, beta_post)
  cat(sprintf("n=%3d: %d heads, posterior mean=%.3f, 95%%CI=[%.3f, %.3f]\n",
              n, x, post_mean, ci[1], ci[2]))
}




With few flips (n=5), the posterior is strongly influenced by the prior — wide and uncertain
As n increases, the posterior narrows and converges toward the true θ = 0.65
The dashed blue line (prior) becomes increasingly irrelevant — data dominates
With n=200, the 95% credible interval barely spans 0.10 units
Practical implication: With informative priors and small samples, Bayesian analysis provides regularization; with large samples, Bayesian and frequentist results converge




Bayesian sequential updating demonstrates a core strength of the framework: each new batch of data refines the posterior, which becomes the prior for the next update
With small samples (n=5), the prior has substantial influence — the posterior is wide and pulled toward the prior mean, providing regularization against noisy estimates
As sample size grows (n=200), the posterior concentrates tightly around the true parameter value and the prior becomes effectively irrelevant
This convergence of Bayesian and frequentist results with large samples is a reassuring property: with enough data, reasonable priors do not bias conclusions

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 9
text:
Credible Intervals vs. Confidence Intervals

Credible IntervalConfidence Interval (Comparison)Highest Density Interval (HDI)Interpretation


A 95% Bayesian credible interval \([a, b]\) means:
\[P(a \leq \theta \leq b \mid \text{data}) = 0.95\]
There is a 95% probability that the true parameter lies in this interval, given the observed data and our prior. This is the direct, intuitive interpretation most people want.
For our coin flip example (posterior Beta(9,5)):


Posterior mean: 0.643 


95% Credible Interval: [ 0.386 , 0.861 ]



Interpretation: There is a 95% probability that the true


probability of heads is between 0.39 and 0.86 given our data and prior.




A 95% frequentist confidence interval \([a, b]\) means:

If we repeated this experiment many times and computed a 95% CI each time, 95% of those intervals would contain the true parameter \(\theta\).

It does NOT mean: “There is a 95% probability that \(\theta\) is in \([a, b]\).”
The parameter \(\theta\) is fixed (but unknown) in the frequentist framework — it either is or isn’t in any given interval. The probability refers to the procedure, not the parameter.








Property
Credible Interval
Confidence Interval




Framework
Bayesian
Frequentist


θ treated as
Random variable (has distribution)
Fixed unknown constant


Statement
P(θ ∈ [a,b] | data) = 0.95
95% of such intervals cover θ


Direct probability on θ?
✓ Yes
✗ No


Depends on prior?
✓ Yes
✗ No


Numeric values
Often very similar (large n)
Often very similar (large n)





A Highest Density Interval (HDI) is a special type of credible interval containing the most probable values:

The HDI is the shortest interval containing 95% of the posterior probability
For symmetric posteriors, the HDI equals the equal-tailed CI
For skewed posteriors, the HDI is preferred — it always contains the mode













Bayesian credible intervals provide direct probability statements about parameters (“95% probability theta is in this range”), while frequentist confidence intervals describe the procedure’s long-run coverage
For large samples, credible and confidence intervals typically give nearly identical numerical results — the distinction matters most conceptually and with small samples
The Highest Density Interval (HDI) is preferred for skewed posteriors because it always contains the mode and is the shortest interval covering 95% of the posterior mass
The ability to make direct probability statements about parameters is one of the primary practical advantages of the Bayesian framework for applied researchers

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 9
text:
Conjugate Prior Distributions
A conjugate prior is a prior distribution that, when combined with a specific likelihood, produces a posterior in the same family as the prior. This makes Bayesian updating analytically tractable — no numerical integration needed.

Beta-Binomial (Proportion)Normal-Normal (Mean)Gamma-Poisson (Count Rate)Why Conjugacy MattersInterpretation


Setting: Estimating a proportion \(\theta\) (e.g., probability of biocompatibility)
\[\text{Prior:} \quad \theta \sim \text{Beta}(\alpha, \beta)\] \[\text{Likelihood:} \quad X \mid \theta \sim \text{Binomial}(n, \theta)\] \[\text{Posterior:} \quad \theta \mid X \sim \text{Beta}(\alpha + X, \;\beta + n - X)\]
Interpretation of hyperparameters: - \(\alpha\) = prior successes (e.g., \(\alpha=2\) imaginary prior “biocompatible” observations) - \(\beta\) = prior failures - Posterior mean = \(\frac{\alpha + X}{\alpha + \beta + n}\) — a weighted average of prior and observed proportions

# Prior: Beta(2, 2) — weak belief in fairness
# Data: 7 biocompatible out of 10 scaffolds
alpha_post <- 2 + 7   # = 9
beta_post  <- 2 + 3   # = 5
cat("Posterior mean:", alpha_post / (alpha_post + beta_post))
cat("95% CI:", qbeta(c(0.025, 0.975), alpha_post, beta_post))



Setting: Estimating a mean \(\mu\) (e.g., mean cell growth, mean scaffold porosity)
\[\text{Prior:} \quad \mu \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_0, \tau_0^2)\] \[\text{Likelihood:} \quad y_i \mid \mu \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2) \quad (\sigma^2 \text{ known})\] \[\text{Posterior:} \quad \mu \mid \mathbf{y} \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(\frac{\frac{\mu_0}{\tau_0^2} + \frac{n\bar{y}}{\sigma^2}}{\frac{1}{\tau_0^2} + \frac{n}{\sigma^2}},\; \left(\frac{1}{\tau_0^2} + \frac{n}{\sigma^2}\right)^{-1}\right)\]
The posterior mean is a precision-weighted average of the prior mean and the observed sample mean — larger \(n\) gives more weight to the data.


Setting: Estimating a rate \(\lambda\) (e.g., bacterial colony count rate)
\[\text{Prior:} \quad \lambda \sim \text{Gamma}(a, b)\] \[\text{Likelihood:} \quad X \mid \lambda \sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda)\] \[\text{Posterior:} \quad \lambda \mid X_1{,}\ldots{,}X_n \sim \text{Gamma}\!\left(a + \sum X_i,\; b + n\right)\]
The posterior mean \(= (a + \sum X_i)/(b + n)\) — a shrinkage estimator that pulls the observed rate toward the prior.









Benefit
Description




Analytic solution
No MCMC sampling needed — exact posterior in closed form


Interpretable updates
Clear “prior counts + data counts” structure


Fast computation
Instant updates as new data arrives (sequential Bayesian)


Good for teaching
Makes Bayesian mechanics transparent



Limitation: Conjugate families are limited. Real-world models with multiple parameters, non-linear effects, or hierarchical structure require MCMC samplers like Stan (used by brms).



Conjugate priors produce closed-form posteriors, avoiding the need for computationally expensive MCMC sampling
The Beta-Binomial conjugate pair is the most intuitive: prior hyperparameters \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\) act as pseudo-counts of prior successes and failures, and simply add to the observed counts
In the Normal-Normal case, the posterior mean is a precision-weighted average — when sample size is large, data dominate; when small, the prior stabilizes the estimate
Conjugacy is elegant but limited to simple models; complex real-world analyses (hierarchical models, non-linear effects) require MCMC samplers like Stan

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 9
section: Bayesian Linear Regression: Concept and Equations
text:
Bayesian Linear Regression: Concept and Equations
In frequentist linear regression, we estimate \(\beta\) by minimizing RSS. In the Bayesian framework, every parameter has a prior distribution, and we update it to a posterior distribution after observing data.

The ModelLaTeXPrior ChoicesOutputInterpretation


Simple linear regression (one predictor, one response):
\[y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i + \epsilon_i, \quad \epsilon_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)\]
Bayesian formulation — specify priors:
\[\beta_0 \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_{\beta_0},\; \sigma_{\beta_0}^2), \qquad \beta_1 \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_{\beta_1},\; \sigma_{\beta_1}^2), \qquad \sigma \sim \text{HalfCauchy}(0, \tau)\]
Posterior (Bayes’ theorem):
\[P(\beta_0, \beta_1, \sigma \mid \mathbf{y}, \mathbf{x}) \propto \underbrace{P(\mathbf{y} \mid \beta_0, \beta_1, \sigma, \mathbf{x})}_{\text{Likelihood}} \times \underbrace{P(\beta_0)P(\beta_1)P(\sigma)}_{\text{Priors}}\]
The posterior is a joint distribution over all three parameters — we can summarize it with marginal posteriors for each.


y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i + \epsilon_i, \quad \epsilon_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)

\beta_j \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_j, \sigma_j^2)

P(\boldsymbol{\beta}, \sigma \mid \mathbf{y}) \propto 
  P(\mathbf{y} \mid \boldsymbol{\beta}, \sigma) \times P(\boldsymbol{\beta}) P(\sigma)


Weakly informative priors (default in brms):








Parameter
Prior
Rationale




\(\beta_0\) (intercept)
\(\mathcal{N}(0, 10)\)
Centered at 0, wide — minimal constraint


\(\beta_j\) (slopes)
\(\mathcal{N}(0, 5)\)
Weakly regularizing — shrinks large slopes


\(\sigma\)
HalfCauchy(0, 2)
Heavy-tailed — allows large residual SD



Weakly informative priors are not flat (uninformative) — they encode the belief that extreme values are unlikely, providing mild regularization without strong influence on the posterior when data are abundant.














In Bayesian linear regression, each parameter gets a full posterior distribution rather than a single point estimate, capturing the complete uncertainty about that parameter
The posterior is proportional to the likelihood times the prior — with sufficient data, the likelihood dominates and results converge to the frequentist estimates
Weakly informative priors (e.g., \(\mathcal{N}(0, 5)\) for slopes) provide mild regularization without strongly influencing results, preventing extreme estimates while letting data speak
The 95% credible intervals from the posterior histograms are directly interpretable: “there is a 95% probability the true parameter lies in this range”

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 9
text:
When to Use Bayesian Methods






Consider Bayesian When:



Prior information exists — previous studies, expert knowledge
Small sample sizes — priors stabilize estimates
Complex models — hierarchical models, missing data
Direct probability statements — “90% chance effect is positive”
Sequential updating — accumulating evidence over time




R Packages for Bayesian Analysis:

brms: Flexible Bayesian models with formula syntax similar to lm/glm (recommended)
rstanarm: Easy Bayesian regression with Stan backend
BayesFactor: Bayesian hypothesis testing

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 9
text:
Bayesian Regression with brms: Setup
We return to the scaffold cell growth dataset (150 scaffolds with porosity, fiber diameter, and crosslink density). We previously fit this with a regression tree and a random forest. Now we fit the same model within the Bayesian framework using brms.
Why brms for this dataset? - We have prior domain knowledge: cell growth should increase with crosslink density (positive slope expected) - We can express this as a weakly informative prior: \(\beta_{\text{crosslink}} \sim \mathcal{N}(2, 2)\) — centered positive but uncertain - We get probability statements: “What is P(porosity effect > 0)?” — not possible in frequentist framework - We get full posterior predictive distributions for new scaffolds — not just point predictions
Key differences from frequentist (integrated):








Feature
Frequentist (lm)
Bayesian (brms)




Parameter estimates
Point estimates
Full posterior distributions


Uncertainty
Confidence intervals
Credible intervals


Interpretation
“95% of such CIs cover true value”
“95% probability θ is in this range”


Prior knowledge
Not used
Explicitly encoded as priors


Model complexity
AIC/BIC for comparison
WAIC, LOO-CV


Predictions
Point ± SE
Full predictive distribution


First-run speed
Fast
Slow (Stan compiles); subsequent: fast

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 9
section: Bayesian Regression with brms: Fitting and Interpretation
text:
Bayesian Regression with brms: Fitting and Interpretation

CodePosterior VisualizationInterpreting the OutputInterpretation



library(brms)

# Frequentist reference model
freq_model <- lm(cell_growth ~ porosity + fiber_diameter + crosslink_density,
                 data = scaffold_reg)
summary(freq_model)

# Bayesian model — nearly identical formula syntax
bayes_model <- brm(
  cell_growth ~ porosity + fiber_diameter + crosslink_density,
  data    = scaffold_reg,
  family  = gaussian(),
  prior   = c(
    prior(normal(0, 5),   class = "b"),           # Weakly informative slopes
    prior(normal(2, 2),   class = "b",            # Positive prior for crosslink
          coef = "crosslink_density"),
    prior(normal(5, 10),  class = "Intercept"),   # Wide intercept prior
    prior(cauchy(0, 2),   class = "sigma")        # Half-Cauchy for residual SD
  ),
  chains = 4, iter = 2000, warmup = 1000,
  seed = 42, cores = 4
)

summary(bayes_model)         # Posterior means, SDs, credible intervals




# Plot posterior distributions of all parameters
plot(bayes_model)

# Posterior predictive check — does model generate realistic data?
pp_check(bayes_model, ndraws = 50)

# Extract posterior draws
draws <- as_draws_df(bayes_model)

# Probability that porosity effect is positive
cat("P(porosity effect > 0) =", mean(draws$b_porosity > 0))

# Probability that crosslink density is strongest predictor
cat("P(|crosslink| > |porosity|) =",
    mean(abs(draws$b_crosslink_density) > abs(draws$b_porosity)))

# 95% Credible intervals for all coefficients
posterior_summary(bayes_model)



The summary(bayes_model) output gives for each parameter:

Estimate: Posterior mean — analogous to the frequentist point estimate
Est.Error: Posterior standard deviation — analogous to the standard error
l-95% CI, u-95% CI: 95% credible interval — directly interpretable as probability statement
Rhat: Convergence diagnostic — values close to 1.00 indicate MCMC chains converged
Bulk ESS / Tail ESS: Effective sample size — should be > 400 for reliable inference

With weak priors and n=150, the Bayesian and frequentist estimates will be very similar. The advantage of brms shows when: (1) the sample is small, (2) we have meaningful prior information, or (3) we need probability statements about parameters.



The brms package lets you specify Bayesian regression models with the same formula syntax as lm(), making the transition from frequentist to Bayesian straightforward
Posterior credible intervals give direct probability statements about parameters (e.g., “95% probability the slope is between 1.2 and 3.4”), unlike frequentist confidence intervals
Specifying an informative prior for crosslink density (\(\mathcal{N}(2, 2)\)) encodes domain knowledge that the effect should be positive, providing regularization especially with small samples
Convergence diagnostics (Rhat near 1.00, ESS > 400) must be checked before interpreting results — poor convergence means the posterior samples are unreliable

Overview







Pre-requisite Material


This appendix introduces the fundamental computing concepts that underlie all of the statistical and data analysis tools used in this course. Understanding hardware, operating systems, and computing architectures will help you make informed decisions about how to run your analyses.

title: Appendix A - Computer Systems & Computing Fundamentals
text:
What is a Computer System?



Hardware: Physical components
Software: Programs and instructions
Firmware: Low-level software in hardware
Data: Information being processed





Computer Components

title: Appendix A - Computer Systems & Computing Fundamentals
text:
Central Processing Unit (CPU)


Core Components

Control Unit (CU): Directs operations
Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU): Performs calculations
Registers: Ultra-fast temporary storage
Cache: High-speed memory buffer


Key Concepts

Clock Speed: Cycles per second (GHz)
Cores: Independent processing units
Threads: Virtual cores for parallel processing
Instruction Set: CPU’s language (x86, ARM)

title: Appendix A - Computer Systems & Computing Fundamentals
text:
Graphics Processing Unit (GPU)


Core Components

Streaming Multiprocessors (SMs): Processing clusters
CUDA Cores/Stream Processors: Parallel compute units
Video Memory (VRAM): Dedicated high-bandwidth memory
Memory Controller: Manages data flow to/from VRAM


Key Concepts

Parallel Architecture: Thousands of cores for simultaneous tasks
Memory Bandwidth: Data throughput (GB/s)
Compute Units: Groups of processing cores
APIs: Programming interfaces (CUDA, OpenCL, Vulkan)

title: Appendix A - Computer Systems & Computing Fundamentals
text:
CPU vs GPU Comparison


CPU Characteristics

Design Focus: Sequential processing & complex tasks
Core Count: 4–64 powerful cores
Architecture: Optimized for single-thread performance
Best For:

Operating systems & general computing
Complex branching logic
Serial tasks



GPU Characteristics

Design Focus: Parallel processing & high throughput
Core Count: Thousands of smaller cores
Architecture: Optimized for massive parallelism
Best For:

Graphics rendering
Machine learning/AI
Scientific simulations
Parallel computations

title: Appendix A - Computer Systems & Computing Fundamentals
text:
Random Access Memory (RAM)


Types of RAM
DRAM (Dynamic RAM)

Main system memory
Needs constant refresh
Cheaper, higher density
DDR4, DDR5 standards

SRAM (Static RAM)

Used in CPU cache
No refresh needed
Faster but expensive


Key Characteristics

Volatile: Data lost when power off
Random Access: Any location equally fast
Bandwidth: Data transfer rate
Latency: Access delay
Dual Channel: Parallel data paths

title: Appendix A - Computer Systems & Computing Fundamentals
text:
Persistent Storage Systems


Hard Disk Drives (HDD)

Mechanical spinning platters
Magnetic storage
Large capacity, low cost
~100–200 MB/s throughput
Higher latency (~10ms)


Solid State Drives (SSD)

No moving parts (NAND Flash)
Electronic storage
Faster access (~0.1ms)
500–7000 MB/s throughput
More expensive per GB

title: Appendix A - Computer Systems & Computing Fundamentals
text:

Input Devices

Keyboard & Mouse
Touchscreen
Microphone & Camera
Sensors
Network Interface


Output Devices

Monitor/Display
Printer
Speakers
Network Interface
Actuators

title: Appendix A - Computer Systems & Computing Fundamentals
text:
What is an Operating System?
An OS is system software that manages computer hardware, software resources, and provides common services for computer programs.


Key Roles

Resource Manager
Extended Machine
User Interface Provider
Security Enforcer


Common Operating Systems

Desktop: Windows, macOS, Unix, Linux
Mobile: Android, iOS
Server: Unix, Linux, Windows Server
Specialized: Supercomputers, IoT

## Core OS Functions {.smaller}

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
***Process Management***

-   Process Creation & Termination
-   Process Scheduling: CPU time allocation
-   Inter-Process Communication (IPC)
-   Synchronization
-   Deadlock Handling

***File System Management***

-   File Operations: Create, read, write, delete
-   Directory Structure
-   Access Control & Permissions
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
***Memory Management***

-   Memory Allocation/Deallocation
-   Virtual Memory
-   Paging & Segmentation
-   Memory Protection
-   Swap Space Management

***Device Management***

-   Device Drivers: Hardware abstraction
-   I/O Scheduling
-   Buffering & Caching
-   Interrupt Handling
:::
:::::

## CPU Scheduling {.smaller}

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Scheduling Algorithms**

-   **First-Come-First-Served (FCFS)**: Simple but can cause long waits
-   **Shortest Job First (SJF)**: Optimal average wait time
-   **Round Robin (RR)**: Fair time slices for all
-   **Priority Scheduling**: Important tasks first
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Performance Metrics**

-   **CPU Utilization**: Keep CPU busy
-   **Throughput**: Jobs completed/time
-   **Turnaround Time**: Total completion time
-   **Waiting Time**: Time in ready queue
-   **Response Time**: First response delay
:::
:::::

## Security & Protection {.smaller}

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Protection Mechanisms**

-   Access Control Lists (ACLs)
-   User/Group Permissions
-   Memory Protection: Segmentation
-   CPU Modes: User vs Kernel
-   Sandboxing: Isolate processes
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Security Features**

-   Authentication: User verification
-   Encryption: Data protection
-   Firewall: Network protection
-   Updates: Patch vulnerabilities
:::
:::::

> "Every program and user should operate using the least amount of privilege necessary"

## Local Computing: Pros and Cons {.smaller}

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Advantages

-   **Low Latency**: No network overhead
-   **Data Control**: Complete ownership
-   **Privacy**: Data never leaves premises
-   **Predictable Costs**: One-time investment
-   **Offline Capability**: Works without internet
-   **Customization**: Full hardware/software control
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
### Disadvantages

-   **Limited Scale**: Hardware constraints
-   **Maintenance**: User responsibility
-   **Upfront Costs**: High initial investment
-   **Disaster Recovery**: Manual backup needed
-   **Resource Waste**: Idle during off-hours
-   **Upgrade Complexity**: Physical intervention
:::

title: Appendix A - Computer Systems & Computing Fundamentals
section: Why Do We Need Coding and Scripting?
text:
Why Do We Need Coding and Scripting?

It is incredibly fast and powerful, particularly for repeated actions
It allows you to do thousands of ‘clicks’ with single commands
Ability to analyze large datasets that Excel and other GUIs can’t handle well
Access to thousands of free programs made for and by scientists
The commands work almost identically across platforms
Ability to use computer clusters like Talapas

title: Appendix A - Computer Systems & Computing Fundamentals
text:
Coding vs. Scripting

coding generally involves computer languages that use compilers

C++, Fortran, Basic, etc.

scripting generally involves computer languages that are interpreted on the fly

Unix, Python, R, Julia, etc.

Coding — faster but less flexible; Scripting — flexible but slower
The distinction has become somewhat fuzzy and most modern analytical pipelines contain a combination of both

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text:
Key dplyr Verbs

dpylr is a package in the tidyverse that gives you functions (aka verbs) to wrangle data.









Verb
Purpose
Example




filter()
Subset rows by values
filter(df, x > 5)


select()
Subset columns by name
select(df, col1, col2)


arrange()
Reorder rows
arrange(df, x)


mutate()
Create new columns
mutate(df, z = x + y)


summarise()
Collapse to summary
summarise(df, mean = mean(x))

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text:
Filter, Arrange, and Select

OutputCodeInterpretation




# A tibble: 55,403 × 19
    year month   day dep_time sched_dep_time dep_delay arr_time sched_arr_time
   <int> <int> <int>    <int>          <int>     <dbl>    <int>          <int>
 1  2013    11     1        5           2359         6      352            345
 2  2013    11     1       35           2250       105      123           2356
 3  2013    11     1      455            500        -5      641            651
 4  2013    11     1      539            545        -6      856            827
 5  2013    11     1      542            545        -3      831            855
 6  2013    11     1      549            600       -11      912            923
 7  2013    11     1      550            600       -10      705            659

 9  2013    11     1      554            600        -6      826            827
10  2013    11     1      554            600        -6      749            751
# ℹ 55,393 more rows
# ℹ 11 more variables: arr_delay <dbl>, carrier <chr>, flight <int>,
#   tailnum <chr>, origin <chr>, dest <chr>, air_time <dbl>, distance <dbl>,
#   hour <dbl>, minute <dbl>, time_hour <dttm>





# Filter rows
filter(flights, month == 11 | month == 12)

# Arrange rows
arrange(flights, year, month, day)

# Select columns
select(flights, year, month, day)




filter() subsets rows based on logical conditions; here selecting November and December flights using the OR operator
arrange() reorders rows by one or more columns (ascending by default)
select() picks specific columns, reducing the tibble to only the variables you need

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text:
Mutate and Transmute

OutputCodeInterpretation




# A tibble: 8 × 3
  flight  gain speed
   <int> <dbl> <dbl>
1   1545     9  370.
2   1714    16  374.
3   1141    31  408.
4    725   -17  517.
5    461   -19  394.
6   1696    16  288.
7    507    24  404.
8   5708   -11  259.





# Add new columns based on existing ones
mutate(flights,
  gain = arr_delay - dep_delay,
  hours = air_time / 60,
  gain_per_hour = gain / hours
)

# Using pipes
flights |>
  mutate(
    gain = arr_delay - dep_delay,
    speed = distance / air_time * 60
  )




mutate() adds new columns while keeping all existing columns, enabling derived variables like “gain” (difference between arrival and departure delay)
The pipe operator |> chains operations together, reading left-to-right like a sentence
New columns can reference other new columns created in the same mutate() call (e.g., gain_per_hour uses gain and hours)

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text:
Group By and Summarise

OutputCodeInterpretation




# A tibble: 365 × 4
# Groups:   year, month [12]
    year month   day delay
   <int> <int> <int> <dbl>
 1  2013     1     1 11.5 
 2  2013     1     2 13.9 
 3  2013     1     3 11.0 
 4  2013     1     4  8.95
 5  2013     1     5  5.73
 6  2013     1     6  7.15
 7  2013     1     7  5.42
 8  2013     1     8  2.55
 9  2013     1     9  2.28
10  2013     1    10  2.84
# ℹ 355 more rows





# Group by categorical variable
by_day <- group_by(flights, year, month, day)

# Summarise within groups
summarise(by_day, delay = mean(dep_delay, na.rm = TRUE))




group_by() splits the data into groups defined by one or more categorical variables (here: year, month, day)
summarise() then collapses each group into a single row with the computed summary statistic
The na.rm = TRUE argument is essential because missing values would otherwise cause the mean to return NA for any group containing missing data










Warning


Aggregation functions return NA if any input value is NA. Use na.rm = TRUE to remove missing values.

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2

Scatterplots

OutputCodeInterpretation














ggplot(mpg, aes(displ, hwy, color = class)) +
  geom_point(size = 4, alpha = 0.6)




The scatterplot reveals a negative relationship between engine displacement and highway fuel efficiency
Color coding by vehicle class shows that compact and subcompact cars cluster at low displacement/high efficiency, while SUVs and pickups cluster at high displacement/low efficiency
The alpha transparency helps reveal overlapping points and density patterns

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text: Boxplots (SVG)

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
section: DRAFT: Boxplot Anatomy with Normal Distribution
text:
DRAFT: Boxplot Anatomy with Normal Distribution

OutputCodeInterpretation














x <- seq(-4, 4, length.out = 1000)
y <- dnorm(x)
q1 <- qnorm(0.25)
q3 <- qnorm(0.75)
iqr <- q3 - q1

layout(matrix(c(1, 2), nrow = 2), heights = c(1, 2))

# Top: boxplot
boxplot(rnorm(10000), horizontal = TRUE, ylim = c(-4, 4),
        col = "white", border = "darkred", lwd = 2, axes = FALSE)

# Bottom: normal curve with shaded regions
plot(x, y, type = "n", axes = FALSE, xlab = "", ylab = "")
x_iqr <- x[x >= q1 & x <= q3]
polygon(c(q1, x_iqr, q3), c(0, dnorm(x_iqr), 0),
        col = rgb(0.4, 0.5, 0.8, 0.5), border = NA)
lines(x, y, lwd = 3, col = "darkred")




The boxplot components map directly to regions of the normal distribution: the box spans Q1 to Q3 (the central 50% of data)
Whiskers extend to Q1-1.5IQR and Q3+1.5IQR, capturing approximately 99.3% of normally distributed data
Points beyond the whiskers are flagged as potential outliers, corresponding to values beyond approximately 2.7 standard deviations from the mean

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text:
Boxplots

OutputCodeInterpretation














ggplot(mpg, aes(manufacturer, hwy, colour = class)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  theme_classic() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust=1))




Boxplots summarize the distribution of highway MPG for each manufacturer, showing median, IQR, and outliers
Color coding by vehicle class reveals that manufacturers producing multiple vehicle types show wider spreads in fuel efficiency
The angled x-axis labels accommodate the many manufacturer names without overlap

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text:
Combining Geoms

OutputCodeInterpretation














ggplot(data=mpg, aes(x=displ, y=hwy)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth() +
  labs(title = "Fuel efficiency decreases with engine size",
       caption = "Data from fueleconomy.gov")




Layering geom_point() and geom_smooth() shows both the raw data and the estimated trend simultaneously
The smoothing curve with confidence band reveals a clear negative trend: larger engines consume more fuel
The gray confidence band widens where data is sparse, honestly representing uncertainty in the trend estimate

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text:
Faceting

OutputCodeInterpretation














ggplot(data=mpg) +
  geom_point(mapping=aes(x=displ, y=hwy)) +
  facet_wrap(~class, nrow=2)




Faceting splits the data into separate panels by vehicle class, making within-group patterns easier to see
Each panel shares the same axes, enabling direct visual comparison of the displacement-efficiency relationship across classes
Some classes (e.g., 2seater) have few data points while others (e.g., SUV) are well-represented, which affects pattern reliability

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text:
Three-Dimensional Data

OutputCodeInterpretation














set.seed(345)
d <- data.frame(a = rnorm(100, 10, 10), b = rnorm(100, 5, 5))
ggplot(d, aes(x=a, y=b)) +
  geom_density2d_filled() +
  theme_minimal()




The filled 2D density plot represents a three-dimensional surface (x, y, density) projected onto two dimensions using color
Warmer/brighter colors indicate regions of higher point density, revealing where observations cluster
This is useful for visualizing bivariate distributions and identifying multimodal patterns that scatterplots alone might miss

Population vs. Sample



Concept
Population
Sample




Definition

Parameters

Goal
What we want to know

Important

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text:
Accuracy vs. Precision







Accuracy: closeness to true value
Precision: closeness of repeated estimates to each other
Replication quantifies variation
Randomization avoids bias


Source: aPriori, Copyright 2004-2007

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text:
Accuracy vs. Precision (SVG)


Accuracy: closeness to true value
Precision: closeness of repeated estimates to each other
Replication quantifies variation
Randomization avoids bias

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text:
Stochastic Processes in Statistics

We often want to know truths about the world, but can only estimate them
Uncertainty in those estimates is a given
Statistics is largely about quantifying and managing uncertainty
Random variables are products of stochastic processes
Expectations are based on theoretical probability distributions

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text:
Two Interpretations of Probability
Frequency interpretation:
“Probabilities are mathematically convenient approximations to long run relative frequencies.”
Subjective (Bayesian) interpretation:
“A probability statement expresses the opinion of some individual regarding how certain an event is to occur.”

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text:
Random Variables and Probability

Probability is the expression of belief in some future outcome
A random variable can take on different values with different probabilities
The sample space is the universe of all possible values
Sample space represented by:

Probability mass distribution (discrete)
Probability density function (continuous)








Important


Probabilities of a sample space always sum to 1.0

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text:
Probability Rules
‘And’ rule (multiplication):

EquationLaTeXInterpretation


\[Pr(X \text{ and } Y) = Pr(X) \times Pr(Y)\]


Pr(X \text{ and } Y) = Pr(X) \times Pr(Y)



The multiplication rule states that the probability of both independent events occurring is the product of their individual probabilities
This means joint events are always less probable than either event alone (since probabilities are between 0 and 1)




‘Or’ rule (addition):

EquationLaTeXInterpretation


\[Pr(X \text{ or } Y) = Pr(X) + Pr(Y)\]


Pr(X \text{ or } Y) = Pr(X) + Pr(Y)



The addition rule gives the probability that at least one of two mutually exclusive events occurs
For non-mutually exclusive events, you must subtract the overlap: Pr(X or Y) = Pr(X) + Pr(Y) - Pr(X and Y)










Note


The ‘and’ rule assumes independent events. For non-independent events, we need conditional probabilities.

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text:
Joint and Conditional Probability
Joint probability (independent events):

EquationLaTeXInterpretation


\[Pr(X,Y) = Pr(X) \times Pr(Y)\]


Pr(X,Y) = Pr(X) \times Pr(Y)



The joint probability of two independent events equals the product of their individual probabilities
Independence means knowing that X occurred gives no information about whether Y will occur
This simple multiplication rule only applies when events are truly independent; for dependent events, conditional probabilities are needed




Conditional probability (independent):

EquationLaTeXInterpretation


\[Pr(Y|X) = Pr(Y) \text{ and } Pr(X|Y) = Pr(X)\]


Pr(Y|X) = Pr(Y) \text{ and } Pr(X|Y) = Pr(X)



For independent events, conditioning on one event does not change the probability of the other
This is the formal definition of statistical independence




Conditional probability (non-independent):

EquationLaTeXInterpretation


\[Pr(Y|X) \neq Pr(Y) \text{ and } Pr(X|Y) \neq Pr(X)\]


Pr(Y|X) \neq Pr(Y) \text{ and } Pr(X|Y) \neq Pr(X)



When events are dependent, knowing that one event occurred changes the probability of the other
Most biological variables are not independent, which is why conditional probability is central to biostatistics

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text:
Likelihood vs. Probability

Probability: proportion of times an event would occur over many trials
Likelihood: conditional probability of a parameter value given data


EquationLaTeXInterpretation


\[L[\text{parameter}|\text{data}] = Pr[\text{data}|\text{parameter}]\]


L[\text{parameter}|\text{data}] = Pr[\text{data}|\text{parameter}]



Likelihood reverses the direction of conditioning: instead of asking “given parameters, how likely is the data?”, it asks “given data, which parameters are most supported?”
The likelihood function uses the same mathematical formula as the probability, but treats the parameter as the variable and the data as fixed
Maximum likelihood estimation finds the parameter value that makes the observed data most probable





Maximum likelihood: highest value of the likelihood function
Bayesian estimate: uses prior distribution to update posterior distribution

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text:
Moments of Distributions
1st moment (mean/expectation):

EquationLaTeXInterpretation


\[E[X] = \sum_{\text{all x}}xP(X=x) = \mu\]


E[X] = \sum_{\text{all x}}xP(X=x) = \mu



The expected value (mean) is a probability-weighted average of all possible outcomes
It represents the “center of mass” of the distribution and the value you would expect on average over many observations
For symmetric distributions the mean equals the median; for skewed distributions they differ




2nd moment (variance):

EquationLaTeXInterpretation


\[Var(X) = E[(X-\mu)^2] = \sigma^2\]


Var(X) = E[(X-\mu)^2] = \sigma^2



Variance measures the average squared deviation from the mean, quantifying how spread out the distribution is
Because deviations are squared, larger deviations contribute disproportionately more to the variance
Variance is in squared units of the original data, which is why we often take the square root to get standard deviation




Standard deviation:

EquationLaTeXInterpretation


\[SD = \sqrt{\sigma^2} = \sigma\]


SD = \sqrt{\sigma^2} = \sigma



The standard deviation is simply the square root of the variance, returning the spread measure to the original units of the data
It quantifies the typical distance of individual observations from the mean




Higher moments include skewness (3rd) and kurtosis (4th).

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
section: PMF, PDF, and CDF in R
text:
PMF, PDF, and CDF in R

OutputCodeInterpretation










Figure 3: Comparing discrete (Poisson) and continuous (Normal) distributions (probability mass function, probability density function, and cumulative density function)








par(mfrow = c(2, 2))

# Discrete (Poisson) PMF
x_disc <- 0:15
plot(x_disc, dpois(x_disc, lambda = 5), type = "h", lwd = 3, col = "steelblue",
     main = "PMF: Poisson(λ=5)", xlab = "x", ylab = "P(X = x)")
points(x_disc, dpois(x_disc, lambda = 5), pch = 19, col = "steelblue")

# Continuous (Normal) PDF
x_cont <- seq(-4, 4, length.out = 200)
plot(x_cont, dnorm(x_cont), type = "l", lwd = 2, col = "coral",
     main = "PDF: Normal(0, 1)", xlab = "x", ylab = "f(x)")

# Poisson CDF
plot(x_disc, ppois(x_disc, lambda = 5), type = "s", lwd = 2, col = "steelblue",
     main = "CDF: Poisson(λ=5)", xlab = "x", ylab = "F(x)")

# Normal CDF
plot(x_cont, pnorm(x_cont), type = "l", lwd = 2, col = "forestgreen",
     main = "CDF: Normal(0, 1)", xlab = "x", ylab = "F(x) = P(X ≤ x)")
abline(h = c(0.025, 0.975), lty = 2, col = "gray")




The PMF (top-left) shows exact probabilities for each discrete outcome in a Poisson distribution; the PDF (top-right) shows probability density for continuous normal values
The CDFs (bottom row) give cumulative probabilities P(X <= x), always increasing from 0 to 1
The dashed lines on the normal CDF mark the 2.5% and 97.5% quantiles, which define the central 95% interval used in hypothesis testing

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text:
Common moments of distributions







Source: Whitlock & Schluter, The Analysis of Biological Data, Table 2.1

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text:
DRAFT: Common moments of distributions
Table 2.1 Common population parameters and sample statistics








Parameter
Statistic
Formula




Mean (\(\mu\))
\(\bar{y}\)
\(\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i}{n}\)


Median
Sample median
\(y_{(n+1)/2}\) if \(n\) odd; \((y_{n/2} + y_{(n/2)+1})/2\) if \(n\) even


Variance (\(\sigma^2\))
\(s^2\)
\(\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{(y_i - \bar{y})^2}{n-1}\)


Standard deviation (\(\sigma\))
\(s\)
\(\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{(y_i - \bar{y})^2}{n-1}}\)


Median absolute deviation (MAD)
Sample MAD
\(\text{median}[\lvert y_i - \text{median} \rvert]\)


Coefficient of variation (CV)
Sample CV
\(\frac{s}{\bar{y}} \times 100\)


Standard error of \(\bar{y}\) (\(\sigma_{\bar{y}}\))
\(s_{\bar{y}}\)
\(\frac{s}{\sqrt{n}}\)


95% confidence interval for \(\mu\)

\(\bar{y} - t_{0.05(n-1)}\frac{s}{\sqrt{n}} \leq \mu \leq \bar{y} + t_{0.05(n-1)}\frac{s}{\sqrt{n}}\)

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text: A Bernouli Trial

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text:
Probability of Independent Events







Source: Whitlock & Schluter, The Analysis of Biological Data

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text:
Bernoulli Distribution
Describes the expected outcome of a single event with probability \(p\).
Example: Flipping a fair coin once
\[Pr(X=\text{Head}) = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5 = p\]
\[Pr(X=\text{Tails}) = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5 = 1 - p = q\]
Probabilities always sum to 1: \(p + q = 1\)

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text:
Let’s Simulate Coin Flips

OutputCodeInterpretation














coin <- c("heads", "tails")
flips <- sample(coin, prob = c(0.5, 0.5), size = 100, replace = TRUE)
barplot(table(flips), col = c("steelblue", "coral"))




The sample() function simulates 100 fair coin flips with equal probability (0.5) for heads and tails
The barplot shows the observed counts, which will be close to but not exactly 50/50 due to random variation
Each run with a different seed will produce slightly different results, illustrating sampling variability

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
section: In-Class Demo: Law of Large Numbers with Coin Flips
text:
In-Class Demo: Law of Large Numbers with Coin Flips
Watch how the proportion of heads converges to 0.5 as we flip more coins:

OutputCodeInterpretation














set.seed(344)
par(mfrow = c(2, 3))

# Different sample sizes
for(n in c(1, 8, 16, 32, 400, 1000)) {
  flips <- rbinom(n = n, size = 1, prob = 0.5)  # 0 = heads, 1 = tails
  prop_heads <- sum(flips == 0) / length(flips)
  prop_tails <- 1 - prop_heads

  barplot(height = c(prop_heads, prop_tails),
          names.arg = c("heads", "tails"),
          col = c("steelblue", "coral"),
          ylim = c(0, 1),
          main = paste("n =", n, "flips"),
          ylab = "Proportion")
  abline(h = 0.5, lty = 2, col = "gray")
}




With very small samples (n=1 or 8), the observed proportion of heads can deviate substantially from 0.5
As sample size increases (n=400, 1000), the observed proportions converge tightly around the true probability of 0.5
This demonstrates the Law of Large Numbers: sample statistics approach population parameters as n grows










Tip


As sample size increases, observed proportions converge to true probability (0.5).

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
section: Law of Large Numbers in R
text:
Law of Large Numbers in R

OutputCodeInterpretation










Figure 4: Sample means converge to population mean as sample size increases








# Simulate cumulative means from a Normal(10, 2) population
set.seed(123)
n <- 1000
samples <- rnorm(n, mean = 10, sd = 2)
cumulative_means <- cumsum(samples) / 1:n

plot(1:n, cumulative_means, type = "l", col = "steelblue", lwd = 2,
     xlab = "Sample Size (n)", ylab = "Cumulative Mean",
     main = "Law of Large Numbers: Mean Converges to μ = 10")
abline(h = 10, col = "red", lwd = 2, lty = 2)
legend("topright", c("Cumulative Mean", "True Mean (μ = 10)"),
       col = c("steelblue", "red"), lwd = 2, lty = c(1, 2))




The cumulative mean starts with high variability when n is small and progressively stabilizes as more samples are added
The blue line converges toward the red dashed line (true mean of 10), demonstrating the Law of Large Numbers
This convergence is the mathematical foundation for why larger sample sizes produce more reliable estimates

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text:
Binomial Distribution
Results from combining several independent Bernoulli events.

EquationLaTeXInterpretation


\[\large f(k) = {n \choose k} p^{k} (1-p)^{n-k}\]


\large f(k) = {n \choose k} p^{k} (1-p)^{n-k}



The binomial formula gives the probability of exactly k successes in n independent Bernoulli trials
The binomial coefficient counts the number of ways to arrange k successes among n trials
The terms p^k and (1-p)^(n-k) give the probability of any one specific arrangement of k successes and n-k failures




Where:

\(n\) = total number of trials
\(k\) = number of successes
\(p\) = probability of success

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text: Binomial Distribution

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text: Binomial Distribution (SVG)

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text:
DRAFT: Binomial Distribution - Bell Shape

OutputCodeInterpretation














# Binomial distribution that produces a bell shape
n <- 20
p <- 0.5
x <- 0:n
probs <- dbinom(x, size = n, prob = p)

barplot(probs, names.arg = x, col = "white", border = "black",
        xlab = "Number of Successes", ylab = "Probability",
        main = "", las = 1, space = 0)




With n=20 trials and p=0.5, the binomial distribution forms a symmetric bell shape centered at 10 successes
This bell shape foreshadows the normal distribution: as n increases, the binomial increasingly resembles a Gaussian curve
The symmetry arises because p=0.5; with unequal p, the distribution would be skewed

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text:
Creating a Binomial Distributions

OutputCodeInterpretation














# Probability of exactly 5 successes in 10 trials
dbinom(x = 5, size = 10, prob = 0.5)

# Plot distribution
plot(0:10, dbinom(x = 0:10, size = 10, prob = 0.5),
     type = "h", lwd = 3, col = "steelblue",
     xlab = "Number of Successes", ylab = "Probability")




The dbinom() function calculates the exact probability of k successes in n independent trials
With 10 fair coin flips (p=0.5), getting exactly 5 heads has the highest probability, but outcomes of 4 or 6 are nearly as likely
The symmetric distribution centered at np=5 reflects the equal probability of success and failure

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text:
Poisson Distribution
For discrete counts (e.g., snails per plot, neuron firings per second).

EquationLaTeXInterpretation


\[Pr(Y=r) = \frac{e^{-\lambda}\lambda^r}{r!}\]


Pr(Y=r) = \frac{e^{-\lambda}\lambda^r}{r!}



The Poisson distribution models the number of events occurring in a fixed interval when events happen independently at a constant rate
It is uniquely defined by a single parameter lambda, which equals both the mean and the variance
Common applications include counting mutations per gene, cells per grid square, or neuron firings per second










Note


The Poisson is a single-parameter distribution: \(\mu = \sigma^2 = \lambda\)
Variables with variance > mean are called “overdispersed” (common in RNA-seq data).

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text:
Poisson Distribution Examples





Gene length by 500 nt bins






Increasing λ values





Source: Whitlock & Schluter, The Analysis of Biological Data

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text:
DRAFT: Poisson Distribution Examples

OutputCodeInterpretation



























# Left panel: gene length distribution (gamma-distributed)
set.seed(42)
gene_lengths <- rgamma(10000, shape = 2.5, rate = 0.001)
gene_lengths <- gene_lengths[gene_lengths <= 15000]

ggplot(data.frame(length = gene_lengths), aes(x = length)) +
  geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(count) / sum(after_stat(count))),
                 binwidth = 500, fill = "#CC6633", color = "#CC6633")

# Right panel: Poisson distributions with different lambda values
x <- 0:20
lambdas <- 1:9
plot(x, dpois(x, lambda = 1), type = "l", lwd = 2, ylim = c(0, 0.45))
for (i in 2:length(lambdas)) {
  lines(x, dpois(x, lambda = lambdas[i]), col = colors[i], lwd = 2)
}




The left panel shows gene length data following a right-skewed (gamma) distribution, which is typical of biological count and size data
The right panel shows how Poisson distributions change with lambda: small lambda values produce highly right-skewed distributions, while larger lambda values produce more symmetric, bell-shaped curves
As lambda increases, the Poisson distribution converges toward a normal distribution, another illustration of the CLT

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text:
Creating Poisson Distributions

OutputCodeInterpretation














# Probability of 2 counts given lambda = 1
dpois(x = 2, lambda = 1)

# Plot distribution
plot(0:15, dpois(x = 0:15, lambda = 3),
     type = "h", lwd = 3, col = "darkgreen",
     xlab = "Count", ylab = "Probability")




The dpois() function calculates the probability of observing exactly r events given a rate lambda
With lambda=3, the distribution peaks near x=3 and is slightly right-skewed, showing that counts near the mean are most probable
The distribution only takes non-negative integer values, reflecting that counts cannot be negative or fractional

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text:
Geometric Distribution
Probability of observing \(k\) trials before the first success:

EquationLaTeXInterpretation


\[P(X=k)=(1-p)^{k-1}p\]


P(X=k)=(1-p)^{k-1}p



The geometric distribution models the number of independent trials until the first success
Like the exponential distribution, it is memoryless: past failures do not affect the probability of future success
With probability p=0.1, the expected number of trials until the first success is 1/p = 10





Mean = \(\frac{1}{p}\)
Variance = \(\frac{(1-p)}{p^2}\)

Example: If extinction probability is 0.1 per year, expected time to extinction?

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text:
Negative Binomial Distribution
Probability of the \(r^{th}\) success on the \(k^{th}\) trial:

EquationLaTeXInterpretation


\[P(X=k)=\binom{k-1}{r-1}p^{r}(1-p)^{k-r}\]


P(X=k)=\binom{k-1}{r-1}p^{r}(1-p)^{k-r}



The negative binomial models the number of trials needed to achieve a fixed number of successes
It generalizes the geometric distribution (which is the special case r=1)
In biology, it is widely used to model overdispersed count data such as RNA-seq read counts, where variance exceeds the mean





Mean = \(\frac{r}{p}\)
Variance = \(\frac{r(1-p)}{p^2}\)

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text:
Continuous Probability Distributions

EquationLaTeXInterpretation


\[P(a\leq X \leq b) = \int_{a}^{b} f(x) dx\]
The indefinite integral sums to one:
\[\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x) dx = 1\]
Expectation: \[E[X] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} xf(x) dx\]


P(a\leq X \leq b) = \int_{a}^{b} f(x) dx

\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x) dx = 1

E[X] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} xf(x) dx



For continuous distributions, probabilities are defined as areas under the curve between two values, not as point probabilities
The total area under the probability density function must equal 1 (certainty)
The expectation (mean) is the integral-weighted average of all possible values

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text:
Uniform Distribution
All outcomes equally probable.

EquationLaTeXInterpretation


\[E[X] = \frac{(a+b)}{2}\]


E[X] = \frac{(a+b)}{2}



The expected value of a uniform distribution is simply the midpoint of the interval [a, b]
This reflects the symmetry: all values are equally likely, so the average must be the center





OutputCodeInterpretation














x <- seq(0, 10, 0.1)
plot(x, dunif(x, 0, 10), type = "l", lwd = 2, col = "purple",
     ylab = "Density", main = "Uniform Distribution (0, 10)")




The flat density line shows that every value between 0 and 10 is equally likely
The density equals 1/(b-a) = 0.1, ensuring the total area under the curve sums to 1
The uniform distribution is often used as a baseline or “uninformative” prior in Bayesian analysis

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text:
Exponential Distribution

EquationLaTeXInterpretation


\[f(x)=\lambda e^{-\lambda x}\]


f(x)=\lambda e^{-\lambda x}



The exponential distribution models waiting times between events in a Poisson process
The rate parameter lambda determines both the mean (1/lambda) and standard deviation (1/lambda)
It is “memoryless”: the probability of an event in the next interval is independent of how long you have already waited





\(E[X] = \frac{1}{\lambda}\)
\(Var(X) = \frac{1}{\lambda^2}\)

Example: If λ equals the instantaneous death rate, the lifespan follows an exponential distribution.

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text:
Exponential Distribution

OutputCodeInterpretation














x <- seq(0, 50, 0.5)
plot(x, dexp(x, rate = 0.1), type = "l", lwd = 2, col = "red",
     ylab = "Density", main = "Exponential Distribution (λ = 0.1)")




The density is highest near zero and decreases exponentially, meaning short waiting times are most probable
With rate=0.1, the expected waiting time is 1/0.1 = 10 units
The long right tail reflects that occasional very long waiting times are possible but increasingly unlikely

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text:
Gamma Distribution
Waiting time until the \(r^{th}\) event at rate \(\lambda\):

EquationLaTeXInterpretation


\[f(x) = \frac{\lambda^r x^{r-1} e^{-\lambda x}}{(r-1)!}\]


f(x) = \frac{\lambda^r x^{r-1} e^{-\lambda x}}{(r-1)!}



The gamma distribution generalizes the exponential to model waiting times until multiple events occur
The shape parameter r controls the number of events; the rate parameter lambda controls how fast events happen
When r=1, the gamma reduces to the exponential distribution





Mean = \(\frac{r}{\lambda}\)
Variance = \(\frac{r}{\lambda^2}\)

Example: Time until 1000 DNA strands synthesized at rate 1/ms.

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text:
Normal (Gaussian) Distribution

EquationLaTeXInterpretation


\[f(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \, \mathrm{e}^{-\frac{(x - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}}\]


f(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \, \mathrm{e}^{-\frac{(x - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}}



The normal (Gaussian) distribution is a symmetric, bell-shaped curve fully described by two parameters: mean and variance
The exponential term creates the bell shape by assigning highest probability density near the mean and decreasing probability as values move further away
Many biological measurements approximate this distribution due to the Central Limit Theorem




Notation: \(v \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)\)

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text: Normal Distribution

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text: Normal Distribution (SVG)

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text:
DRAFT: Normal Distribution - 68-95-99.7 Rule

OutputCodeInterpretation














x <- seq(-4, 4, length.out = 1000)
y <- dnorm(x)

plot(x, y, type = "l", lwd = 3, axes = FALSE,
     xlab = "", ylab = "", ylim = c(-0.02, max(y) * 1.35))

# Shade 99.7% region (3 SD)
x_99 <- x[x >= -3 & x <= 3]
polygon(c(-3, x_99, 3), c(0, dnorm(x_99), 0),
        col = rgb(0.85, 0.85, 0.95, 0.5), border = NA)
# Shade 95% region (2 SD)
x_95 <- x[x >= -2 & x <= 2]
polygon(c(-2, x_95, 2), c(0, dnorm(x_95), 0),
        col = rgb(0.7, 0.7, 0.9, 0.5), border = NA)
# Shade 68% region (1 SD)
x_68 <- x[x >= -1 & x <= 1]
polygon(c(-1, x_68, 1), c(0, dnorm(x_68), 0),
        col = rgb(0.5, 0.5, 0.85, 0.6), border = NA)




The 68-95-99.7 rule states that approximately 68% of data falls within 1 SD of the mean, 95% within 2 SD, and 99.7% within 3 SD
This rule provides quick probability estimates without needing to compute integrals
Values beyond 3 standard deviations are extremely rare (0.3%), which is why they are often flagged as potential outliers

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text: Normal Distribution (SVG)

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text:
DRAFT: Normal Distributions - Varying Parameters

OutputCodeInterpretation














par(mfrow = c(3, 1), mar = c(4, 4, 2, 1))

# Normal with mu=40, sigma=1
x1 <- seq(35, 45, length.out = 300)
plot(x1, dnorm(x1, mean = 40, sd = 1), type = "l", lwd = 2,
     xlab = "x", ylab = "f(x)")

# Normal with mu=10, sigma=5
x2 <- seq(-10, 30, length.out = 300)
plot(x2, dnorm(x2, mean = 10, sd = 5), type = "l", lwd = 2,
     xlab = "x", ylab = "f(x)")

# Normal with mu=50, sigma=3
x3 <- seq(38, 62, length.out = 300)
plot(x3, dnorm(x3, mean = 50, sd = 3), type = "l", lwd = 2,
     xlab = "x", ylab = "f(x)")




Changing the mean shifts the distribution left or right along the x-axis without altering its shape
Changing the standard deviation controls the spread: smaller sigma produces a taller, narrower curve; larger sigma produces a shorter, wider curve
The total area under each curve equals 1, regardless of the parameter values

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
section: Why Normal is Special in Biology
text:
Why Normal is Special in Biology







Source: Francis Galton, 1886; data from Galton’s height study

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
section: DRAFT: Galton’s Regression to the Mean
text:
DRAFT: Galton’s Regression to the Mean

OutputCodeInterpretation














# Recreate Galton's parent-offspring height data (approximate)
set.seed(1885)
n <- 200
parent_height <- rnorm(n, mean = 68, sd = 2)
offspring_height <- 29.4 + 0.57 * parent_height + rnorm(n, 0, 1.5)

# Create bubble-like effect by rounding
parent_r <- round(parent_height)
offspring_r <- round(offspring_height)

df <- data.frame(parent = parent_r, offspring = offspring_r)
df_counts <- as.data.frame(table(df$parent, df$offspring))

plot(df_counts$parent, df_counts$offspring,
     cex = sqrt(df_counts$count) * 1.2,
     pch = 16, col = rgb(0.3, 0.3, 0.3, 0.7),
     xlab = "AVERAGE HEIGHT OF PARENTS (IN)",
     ylab = "HEIGHT OF OFFSPRING (IN)")

# Regression line vs 1:1 line
abline(a = 29.4, b = 0.57, lwd = 2)
abline(a = 0, b = 1, lwd = 2, lty = 2)




The regression line (solid) has a slope of 0.57, less than the 1:1 line (dashed), demonstrating regression to the mean
Tall parents tend to have offspring shorter than themselves; short parents tend to have taller offspring
This phenomenon occurs because extreme values arise partly from chance, and offspring “regress” toward the population mean

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
section: Why Normal is Special in Biology
text:
Why Normal is Special in Biology







Source: Birth data visualization

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text:
Z-Scores of Normal distributions
Standardize variables to mean = 0, SD = 1:

EquationLaTeXInterpretation


\[\huge z_i = \frac{(x_i - \bar{x})}{s}\]


\huge z_i = \frac{(x_i - \bar{x})}{s}



A z-score expresses how many standard deviations a value lies from the mean
Standardizing allows comparison of values from distributions with different means and spreads
The resulting standard normal distribution (mean=0, SD=1) is the basis for many statistical tests




This is the standard normal distribution.

text:

Prefix

p

qnorm(0.975, 0, 1)

Random number generator
rnorm(100, 0, 1)



Works for: binom, pois, exp, norm, geom, nbinom, unif, gamma

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text:
Parameter Estimation

Estimation infers population parameters from sample data
Sample estimates rarely equal population parameters exactly
Sampling distribution: all values we might obtain from samples
Standard error: standard deviation of sampling distribution







Important


NO ESTIMATE IS USEFUL WITHOUT A STANDARD ERROR!

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text:
Estimation Approaches



Approach
Description




Parametric
Assumes specific distributions


Resampling
Bootstrap/randomization for empirical distributions


OLS
Ordinary Least Squares optimization


Maximum Likelihood
Model-based estimates with confidence


Bayesian
Incorporates prior information

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text:
The Central Limit Theorem
For most data, we can’t determine sampling distributions empirically.
The CLT tells us that the sampling distribution of the mean approaches normal as sample size increases, regardless of the underlying distribution.

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text:
Visualizing the Central Limit Theorem

OutputCodeInterpretationTry It










Figure 5: CLT: Sample means become normal even from non-normal populations








par(mfrow = c(2, 4))

# Exponential distribution (skewed)
set.seed(42)
exp_pop <- rexp(10000, rate = 1)
hist(exp_pop, main = "Population\n(Exponential)", col = "lightgray",
     border = "white", xlab = "Value", breaks = 30)

# Sample means for different n
for(n in c(5, 30, 100)) {
  means <- replicate(1000, mean(sample(exp_pop, n, replace = TRUE)))
  hist(means, main = paste("Sample Means\n(n =", n, ")"),
       col = "steelblue", border = "white", xlab = "Mean", breaks = 25)
}

# Uniform distribution
unif_pop <- runif(10000, 0, 1)
hist(unif_pop, main = "Population\n(Uniform)", col = "lightgray",
     border = "white", xlab = "Value", breaks = 30)

for(n in c(5, 30, 100)) {
  means <- replicate(1000, mean(sample(unif_pop, n, replace = TRUE)))
  hist(means, main = paste("Sample Means\n(n =", n, ")"),
       col = "coral", border = "white", xlab = "Mean", breaks = 25)
}




The leftmost panels show the original population distributions: exponential (heavily right-skewed) and uniform (flat)
As sample size increases from n=5 to n=100, the distribution of sample means becomes increasingly bell-shaped regardless of the original distribution
By n=30, the sampling distribution is approximately normal, which is why n=30 is often cited as a practical threshold for CLT to take effect




# Try with a bimodal population - how does the CLT perform?
set.seed(42)
bimodal_pop <- c(rnorm(5000, mean = 3, sd = 1), rnorm(5000, mean = 8, sd = 1))
hist(bimodal_pop, main = "Bimodal Population", col = "lightgray", breaks = 40)

# Sample means at different n
par(mfrow = c(1, 3))
for(n in c(5, 30, 100)) {
  means <- replicate(1000, mean(sample(bimodal_pop, n, replace = TRUE)))
  hist(means, main = paste("Sample Means (n =", n, ")"),
       col = "steelblue", border = "white", breaks = 25)
}
# Question: At what n does the bimodal shape disappear in the sampling distribution?

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
section: CLT in Action: Watch the Sampling Distribution Build
text:
CLT in Action: Watch the Sampling Distribution Build
This animation shows repeated sampling from a skewed exponential population (left). Each sample of n=25 produces a mean, which is added to the histogram on the right. Watch how:

The distribution of means becomes bell-shaped despite the skewed population
The estimate (red line) converges toward the true μ
The SEM (green bracket) shrinks as more samples are drawn








Source: CLT sampling animation created with Python/matplotlib

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text:
CLT in Quantitative Genetics
Why are complex traits normally distributed?
Simulate a trait controlled by 5 genes, each with additive effects:

OutputCodeInterpretation














set.seed(245)
n_individuals <- 100

# Each locus contributes -1, 0, or +1 to phenotype (diploid genotypes)
contributions <- matrix(0, nrow = n_individuals, ncol = 5)
for(locus in 1:5) {
  genotype <- rbinom(n_individuals, size = 2, prob = 0.5)  # 0, 1, or 2 copies
  contributions[, locus] <- ifelse(genotype == 2, 1,
                                    ifelse(genotype == 1, 0, -1))
}

# Sum across loci to get phenotype
phenotype <- rowSums(contributions)

par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
hist(phenotype, col = "steelblue", border = "white",
     main = "5-Locus Trait Distribution",
     xlab = "Phenotype Value", breaks = seq(-6, 6, 1))

# Add environmental noise to make continuous
phenotype_continuous <- phenotype + rnorm(n_individuals, 0, 0.5)
hist(phenotype_continuous, col = "coral", border = "white",
     main = "With Environmental Variation",
     xlab = "Phenotype Value", breaks = 20)




The left panel shows that even with only 5 loci, summing discrete genetic effects produces a roughly bell-shaped distribution
Adding environmental noise (right panel) smooths the distribution into an approximately normal shape
This demonstrates why the CLT explains the normal distribution of complex traits: many small, independent genetic and environmental effects sum to produce a Gaussian outcome










Note


This is why quantitative traits (height, weight, blood pressure) are approximately normal!

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text:
Sampling Variation of a Parameter







Source: Whitlock & Schluter, The Analysis of Biological Data

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text:
DRAFT: Population vs Sampling Distribution

OutputCodeInterpretation














# Simulate gene length data (right-skewed gamma)
set.seed(42)
pop_data <- rgamma(100000, shape = 2.5, rate = 0.001)
pop_data <- pop_data[pop_data <= 15000]
pop_mean <- mean(pop_data)

# Sampling distribution: means of samples of n=100
sample_means <- replicate(10000, mean(sample(pop_data, 100)))

par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(5, 4, 3, 1))

# Left: Population distribution
hist(pop_data, breaks = seq(0, 15000, by = 500),
     col = "#CC6633", border = "#CC6633",
     main = "Population Distribution",
     xlab = "Gene length (no. nucleotides)", ylab = "Probability",
     freq = FALSE, las = 1)

# Right: Sampling distribution
hist(sample_means, breaks = 40,
     col = "#CC6633", border = "#CC6633",
     main = "Sampling Distribution",
     xlab = expression("Sample mean length " * bar(Y) * " (nucleotides)"),
     ylab = "Probability", freq = FALSE, las = 1)




The left panel shows the population distribution of gene lengths, which is right-skewed (gamma-distributed)
The right panel shows the sampling distribution of the mean from samples of n=100, which is approximately normal despite the skewed population
This demonstrates the Central Limit Theorem: the distribution of sample means becomes normal regardless of the shape of the underlying population

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text: Source: Whitlock & Schluter, The Analysis of Biological Data

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text:
DRAFT: Sampling Distribution of Means

OutputCodeInterpretation














set.seed(42)
pop_data <- rgamma(100000, shape = 2.5, rate = 0.001)
pop_data <- pop_data[pop_data <= 15000]
sample_means <- replicate(10000, mean(sample(pop_data, 100)))

par(mfrow = c(2, 1), mar = c(5, 5, 3, 8))

# Top: Sampling distribution
hist(sample_means, breaks = 40,
     col = "#CC6633", border = "#CC6633",
     main = "", xlim = c(1500, 4000),
     xlab = expression("Sample mean length " * bar(X) * " (nucleotides)"),
     ylab = "Probability", freq = FALSE, las = 1)

# Bottom: Single sample mean
plot(1, 1, type = "n", xlim = c(1500, 4000), ylim = c(0, 50),
     xlab = expression("Sample mean length " * bar(X) * " (nucleotides)"),
     ylab = "Frequency", las = 1, main = "")
segments(2411, 0, 2411, 1, lwd = 8, col = "#CC6633")




The top panel shows the sampling distribution: the distribution of means from many samples of size n=100, which is approximately normal even though the population is skewed
The bottom panel shows that any single sample mean is just one value drawn from this sampling distribution
This highlights that our sample estimate is one realization from a distribution of possible estimates

title: Statistics for Bioengineering - Week 2
text:
DRAFT: Population vs Single Sample

OutputCodeInterpretation














set.seed(42)
pop_data <- rgamma(100000, shape = 2.5, rate = 0.001)
pop_data <- pop_data[pop_data <= 15000]
pop_mean <- mean(pop_data)

# Single sample
single_sample <- sample(pop_data, 100)
sample_mean <- round(mean(single_sample))

par(mfrow = c(2, 1), mar = c(5, 5, 3, 10))

# Top: Population distribution
hist(pop_data, breaks = seq(0, 15000, by = 500),
     col = "#CC6633", border = "#CC6633",
     main = "", xlab = "Gene length (number of nucleotides)",
     ylab = "Probability", freq = FALSE, las = 1)

# Bottom: Single sample
hist(single_sample, breaks = seq(0, 15000, by = 500),
     col = "#CC6633", border = "#CC6633",
     main = "", xlim = c(0, 15000),
     xlab = "Gene length (number of nucleotides)",
     ylab = "Frequency", las = 1)




The top panel shows the true population distribution of gene lengths (right-skewed), with the population mean marked
The bottom panel shows what we actually observe: a single sample of 100 genes, whose mean approximates but does not equal the true population mean
This illustrates the fundamental challenge of statistics: we must infer population parameters from limited sample data
